steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Small bowel obstruction (given extensive prior abdominal surgeries including Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and ventral hernia repair with mesh — adhesive SBO or internal hernia), mesenteric ischemia (given prior hypotension requiring vasopressors, paroxysmal afib, and metabolic acidosis with low bicarbonate), post-antibiotic Clostridioides difficile colitis, gynecologic pathology (post-TAH pelvic abscess or ovarian remnant), or mesh-related complication
ensemble.information_gap: The anatomic source and etiology of the acute abdominal process cannot be determined from history, exam, and labs alone — specifically, whether this represents a surgical emergency (internal hernia, SBO, mesenteric ischemia, perforation) versus an infectious/inflammatory process, and which organ system is involved
ensemble.expected_finding: Evidence of internal hernia or adhesive small bowel obstruction (transition point, dilated loops), mesenteric ischemia (pneumatosis, mesenteric stranding, vascular occlusion), intra-abdominal abscess or free air, diverticulitis, or appendicitis — particularly given the Roux-en-Y anatomy and prior mesh repair
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has an undifferentiated acute abdomen with prior hypotension requiring vasopressors, metabolic acidosis (bicarbonate 16), anemia, and a complex surgical history including Roux-en-Y gastric bypass that markedly increases risk for internal hernia — CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the appropriate first-line cross-sectional imaging to rapidly characterize the source and severity.
grounding: History: 'hypotensive and started on leveophed drip for SBPs to the ___'
History: 'multiple prior abdominal surgeries including lap Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, ventral hernia repair w mesh'
History: '4 days of periumbilical and lower abdominal pain... progressively worse'
Physical examination: 'extremely TTP lower abdomen worst in RLQ w voluntary guarding, no rebound'
Laboratory tests: 'Hemoglobin: 9.3 g/dL' and 'Hematocrit: 29.8%' — significant anemia
History: 'paroxysmal afib (on ASA 81 but no anticoagulation)' — thromboembolic risk
History: 'Passing flatus, no BM since yesterday' — partial obstruction not excluded
History: 'completed a course of Macrobid for UTI 12 days ago' — recent antibiotic exposure
ensemble.reasoning: This patient with a complex surgical abdomen (Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, mesh hernia repair) presented with progressive abdominal pain, RLQ tenderness with guarding, and required vasopressors — raising immediate concern for a surgical emergency such as internal hernia, adhesive SBO, or mesenteric ischemia. The metabolic acidosis (bicarb 16) and prior hemodynamic instability suggest a physiologically significant process, and the Roux-en-Y anatomy makes internal hernia a high-priority diagnosis that is notoriously difficult to detect without cross-sectional imaging. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the single most efficient test to simultaneously characterize bowel anatomy, identify obstruction or ischemia, assess for perforation or abscess, and evaluate vascular structures.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: Small bowel obstruction (given extensive prior abdominal surgeries including Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and ventral hernia repair with mesh — adhesive SBO or internal hernia), mesenteric ischemia (given prior hypotension requiring vasopressors, paroxysmal afib, and metabolic acidosis with low bicarbonate), post-antibiotic Clostridioides difficile colitis, gynecologic pathology (post-TAH pelvic abscess or ovarian remnant), or mesh-related complication
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The anatomic source and etiology of the acute abdominal process cannot be determined from history, exam, and labs alone — specifically, whether this represents a surgical emergency (internal hernia, SBO, mesenteric ischemia, perforation) versus an infectious/inflammatory process, and which organ system is involved
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Evidence of internal hernia or adhesive small bowel obstruction (transition point, dilated loops), mesenteric ischemia (pneumatosis, mesenteric stranding, vascular occlusion), intra-abdominal abscess or free air, diverticulitis, or appendicitis — particularly given the Roux-en-Y anatomy and prior mesh repair
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: The patient has an undifferentiated acute abdomen with prior hypotension requiring vasopressors, metabolic acidosis (bicarbonate 16), anemia, and a complex surgical history including Roux-en-Y gastric bypass that markedly increases risk for internal hernia — CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the appropriate first-line cross-sectional imaging to rapidly characterize the source and severity.
grounding: History: 'hypotensive and started on leveophed drip for SBPs to the ___'
History: 'multiple prior abdominal surgeries including lap Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, ventral hernia repair w mesh'
History: '4 days of periumbilical and lower abdominal pain... progressively worse'
Physical examination: 'extremely TTP lower abdomen worst in RLQ w voluntary guarding, no rebound'
Laboratory tests: 'Hemoglobin: 9.3 g/dL' and 'Hematocrit: 29.8%' — significant anemia
History: 'paroxysmal afib (on ASA 81 but no anticoagulation)' — thromboembolic risk
History: 'Passing flatus, no BM since yesterday' — partial obstruction not excluded

representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient with a complex surgical abdomen (Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, mesh hernia repair) presented with progressive abdominal pain, RLQ tenderness with guarding, and required vasopressors — raising immediate concern for a surgical emergency such as internal hernia, adhesive SBO, or mesenteric ischemia. The metabolic acidosis (bicarb 16) and prior hemodynamic instability suggest a physiologically significant process, and the Roux-en-Y anatomy makes internal hernia a high-priority diagnosis that is notoriously difficult to detect without cross-sectional imaging. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the single most efficient test to simultaneously characterize bowel anatomy, identify obstruction or ischemia, assess for perforation or abscess, and evaluate vascular structures.

vindication.actual_finding: CT demonstrated acute appendicitis with a markedly thickened, inflamed appendix measuring up to 10 mm, extensive periappendiceal stranding, and a tiny locule of intraperitoneal gas adjacent to the ascending colon consistent with perforation, in the setting of Roux-en-Y anatomy.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The scan resolved the anatomic source of the acute abdominal process by identifying perforated appendicitis — one of the surgical emergencies explicitly listed in the expected findings — thereby confirming that an urgent surgical etiology was present.
